title: The Macro Minute: When will the BOJ act to tone down volatility in the long end of the JGB market?

Happy Tuesday out there team 42, it's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Tuesday,
April 28th, 2026. As always, we start
with the executive summary from today's
latest morning note, so let's dive right
in. Today's key macro question is, when
will the BOJ act forcefully enough to
tone down volatility in the long end of
the JGB market? Our answer is, in one of
the more hawkish holds you'll ever see,
the Bank of Japan voted 6 to 3 to leave
its policy rates unchanged while
ratcheting up its inflation forecast.
This comes amid intensifying pressure by
the Takeichi government to gain support
for its reflation agenda via Japanese
monetary policy. While our analysis of
hawkish JPY money market and BOJ balance
sheet dynamics signal that Governor Ueda
and his colleagues will accelerate their
normalization of Japanese monetary
policy over the medium term,
the evolving complexion of the BOJ
board, a deteriorating growth outlook,
and pressure from the Takeichi
government suggest they are unlikely to
do whatever it takes, to borrow phrase
from Draghi, to tone down volatility in
the long end of the JGB market anytime
soon.
This has negative implications for US
Treasury bonds and global duration risk
broadly. So, the key takeaway there is
that the Bank of Japan is highly
unlikely to get ahead of the JGB curve
in terms of hawkish policy normalization
in a way that would actually tone down
inflation expectations and and and bond
market volatility in the long end of the
JGB curve. And if they're unwilling to
do whatever it takes, then ultimately we
have to
expect that this sort of underlying bid
for duration risk across global
sovereign debt is going to remain, and
our opinion that's that's naturally
bullish for gold from a longer term
perspective. Obviously, it doesn't
necessarily mean gold has to go up every
single day, but from a longer term
perspective, that is supportive because
it reduces the moneyness of those
securities relative to the core, the
hardest money that is gold.
Transition to our 42 macro dashboard.
As always, we wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
liquidity vacuum in KISS. Says, "Hey
skipper, thanks for building out KISS
and Dr. Mo. This is becoming a core part
of my portfolio. Recently, I've heard
you sound the alarm on the liquidity
vacuum risk that investors are ignoring.
However, the KISS and Dr. Mo remain
unchanged. As 42 macro subscribers,
should we take that as an early warning
risk that we don't need to worry about
it for now? Thanks so much." So, the key
takeaway So, the the short answer is
yes, we don't need to worry about it for
now because the wisdom of the crowd,
which is what KISS and Dr. Mo are
anchoring on, is not yet ready to pivot
to a risk-off market regime or pivot any
of the features the main features in
KISS being stocks, gold, Bitcoin, and
the main asset classes across Dr. Mo to
a negative condition, to to to negative
proper trade signals in Dr. Mo, or to
reduced allocations in KISS. And so,
that ultimately means is that it's the
market has not yet determined that it
wants to price in the left side of the
left, you know, the left the left tail
of the distribution. Reminder that
there's always a full distribution of
probable economic policy and market
outcomes. There's always a full
distribution, the right side of the
distribution, the left side of the
distribution, the middle of the
distribution. The shape of the
distribution evolves over time in terms
of the area under the left side of the
curve or the area under the right side
of the curve. And ultimately, it's the
market's job, it's the wisdom of the
crowd, it's the herd's job to determine
which side of the distribution wants to
price in and when. It's not our job to
determine that. And so, what KISS and
Dr. Mo are designed to do is listen to
the wisdom of the crowd, notice the
behavior of the herd changing faster
than the median investor in the herd or
the median investor in the crowd, so
that we can put our buy orders ahead of
their buy orders or our sell orders
ahead of their sell orders. We want
their flows to make us rich, and we
don't want their flows to make us poor
heading into a risk-off market regime.
And so, just kind of wrapping up
on this, you know, if you just go and
look at our Dr. Mo signal for the S&P
500, you know, you can go back and study
Dr. Mo,
you know, how KISS and Dr. Mo would have
traded any of these events. And so, the
reason I bring up COVID is because COVID
actually was the fastest 30-plus percent
decline in US equity market history. In
the history of the entire US equity
market, this is as fast as it's ever
gone down by 30%. And so, what we can
see here from the perspective of KISS
and Dr. Mo is that we would have been
out and comfortably on the sidelines as
early as the 2nd of March of 2020, and
then back invested in the S&P 500 on the
at the 30th of March on 2020 with a half
position, and then a long max position
on the 13th of April 2020. And so, this
tells you that, you know, KISS and Dr.
Mo when dealing with something as
dealing such a sudden stop risk as a
global pandemic and the global liquidity
crisis that this global pandemic
produced, KISS and Dr. Mo did an
exceptionally
exceptional job in terms of risk
management that. So, I want investors in
our community to understand that, hey,
the reason we designed our risk
management systems the way they are in
terms of featuring volatility targeting
and and dynamic position sizing is
because that mimics the
risk management systems of the top funds
across global Wall Street, which, you
know, tend to produce results like this
for their clients as well. So, we're
wrapping it there. Darius Dale here
presenting our macro minute for Tuesday,
April 28th, 2026. Best of luck out there
today. We'll catch back here tomorrow.
Cheers.
